title: Christopher Manning: Large Language Models in 2025 – How Much Understanding and Intelligence? - YouTube
text:
So welcome everyone
on what is perhaps
the worst weather day
in the California Bay
area for the entire year.
But I'm delighted to be here
to speak to you all today,
and to give somewhat of an
introduction to large language
models in 2025, talking
about how much understanding
and intelligence that they show.
I'm actually one of
those both sides people
that Leila was
already mentioning.
I have a PhD in linguistics,
but somehow the Stanford School
of Engineering has always been
nice at letting me pretend
to be an engineer as well.
So for artificial
intelligence, it's
a field with an
extremely long history.
And actually we've got
Ed Feigenbaum here today
who is certainly a
representative from the very
earliest days of that history.
So there have been 60 plus
years of artificial intelligence
research and actually a year
or so ago, we put together
a great little video for which
we got an Emmy award on AI
at Stanford, which you
can find on YouTube.
So if you want to find out
about the history of AI,
I encourage you to go
and give that a watch.
But I think it's fair to say
that for so much of the world,
AI seem to appear out of
nowhere with an enormous thud
a couple of years ago, and that
was when OpenAI's ChatGPT was
released, and
suddenly discussion
of ChatGPT and
artificial intelligence
was appearing on all the major
news shows and large magazine
spreads and all of
those sorts of things.
And that then also caused these
extremely rapid inflections
in the corporate world.
So it suddenly became
mandated that every S&P 500
CEO had to talk about AI on
their quarterly earnings call,
and all the usual
kinds of people
started making
predictions, with McKinsey
saying that generative AI
could add 2.6 to $4.4 trillion
annually to the economy.
And Goldman Sachs saying that
AI would raise global GDP
by 7% in the next 10 years.
And there may be exuberance,
but there was a reality
that people were just shocked by
an amazing new capability that
was just way beyond certainly in
terms of its sense of language,
understanding and
generation and understanding
of the world, anything that
they'd ever seen before.
So to give a sense
of this, here's
my cute little example
for this morning.
I've asked ChatGPT to say, write
a sonnet praising Wikipedia
in which each line
begins with the letter W.
And this is what
it came up with.
With wisdom vast, it
spans the world so wide.
Wielding the wealth of
knowledge free to share.
Words weave through time.
Where curious minds confide.
Whispering truths with patience,
just and fair, dot, dot.
Wisdom and will sustain
this noble dream.
Wikipedia our light,
our endless stream.
Now, this is maybe not
the finest piece of poetry
ever written, but it's amazing.
You can give it
weird instructions
like start each line
with the letter W,
it understands them
perfectly and it delivers.
And for those of you
whose high school
education was a bit rusty
by now, write sonnets.
They're 14 lines and have an
A, B, A, B rhyming pattern
and that they're in iambic
pentameter with wisdom vast.
It spans the world so
wide, it's totally done it.
And this appeared on my
screen in three seconds
when I asked it to.
You couldn't get a
human poet to produce--
well, they'd probably
have difficulty
coming up with a sonnet in
which every line starts with a W
period, but you
certainly wouldn't get it
in three seconds.
So there is something
quite amazing here.
And it does just have a lot of
details there about the world.
So that was an amazing.
So let me just have a bit of
historical stuff about how
we got to here.
So the starting point
was this breakthrough
in large language models.
And large language
models initially
build on an extremely
simple idea.
It gets dressed up as
unsupervised or self-supervised
learning over enormous amounts
of existing pieces of text,
and all you do is ask
your large neural network
to play a game where you
say, OK, the text so far
is Ford plans to.
And can you guess what the
next word is going to be?
Where of course, since we
have the whole article,
we know what the next
word is going to be.
So maybe it's open,
announce, close.
Something like that
would seem appropriate.
So in other words, we're
asking our large language model
to play Mad Libs
like kids often do.
So this is the audience
participation section
so we can know that we're
intelligent human beings.
What happens when
a unicorn poops?
Unicorns aren't
like other animals.
They're magical
perhaps, they look
like horses with hooves for feet
and are something mane of hair.
Colored mane of hair,
flowing mane of hair.
OK.
So we're all intelligent
and we can play this game.
And the amazing
thing is that if you
take a very large neural
network with a ton of storage
space inside it, and you
feed into it a very, very
large amount of text
and you do nothing else,
out comes is large
language model, which
has an amazing ability
to generate fluent text
and seems to understand
a lot about the world.
And this was a complete
change in direction.
This wasn't how
artificial intelligence
was meant to be achieved.
This seems so dumb
on the face of it.
We're just predicting the next
word in turn from each context.
And so it takes a little bit
of thinking to think, well,
how could you get anything
that seems knowledge
of language, knowledge of the
world intelligence out of this?
But if you think about it a
bit more, you can get the idea.
If I do the knowledge
case, you're
going to have these contexts
like the French President.
And if you know the name
of the French President
and you can put Macron
after it, you're
going to be better
at predicting.
So it's good for it to
learn that knowledge.
And then if you start training
your model on a few more
100 million words
of text, some of it
is going to be in
different decades.
And so maybe, if you
will start to learn
that if earlier in the
sentence, it said in 1980
or something like that,
you shouldn't say Macron,
you should say, was
that Mitterrand?
Mitterrand.
So you start to
learn about history
and you can predict people's
political views and everything.
And so this is what
these models start to do.
And so in the late two--
this is all very recent.
But in the late 2010s, they
started off this model where we
could build bigger and
bigger large language models
by throwing more compute, more
data and more dollars at them.
And as you did that, performance
in terms of text generation
and understanding of the world
just seemed to go up and up.
And so that was the playbook of
the first era of large language
models.
Just throw more stuff into
ever bigger computer clusters
and amazing in
progress would be made.
Indeed, it seemed like we got
a qualitative shift in what
emerges that we no longer seem
to have a simple statistical
predict the next word language
model, which it actually existed
since about 1980 and were
used for things like spelling
correction and
machine translation.
But rather we seem to have
a machine that understands
language and the world.
Just to give you a sense
of scale, if you're not
on up on all the
latest numbers, here's
a recent large open
source language model,
Meta's LLaMA 3.1.
So it was trained on 15.6
trillion tokens, roughly words.
That's a lot of books.
It has an architecture
where there
are 405 billion parameters.
So this unit parameters are
individual floating point
numbers in the
neural network that
are used to encode knowledge
of language in the world.
And it's trained for about two
months on 16,000 NVIDIA each 100
GPUs.
The state of the art GPUs
at the time it was done,
and it's these NVIDIA GPUs with
their massive floating point
compute that have
been so essential
to these recent developments
in artificial intelligence.
So that was great,
but it didn't actually
do everything that we wanted.
Because it gave fantastic
language generation.
But it didn't become a household
name like ChatGPT in late 2022.
And the reason for that, is
that it generates some text
after a piece of text, but not
the piece of text you wanted.
So if you ask GPT-3 who the
US President is by giving it
the words, who is
the US president,
it generates some
appropriate text after that,
but it might say, who is
the vice president, what
are the levels of
government, what
is the difference between
the level of blur.
It's not really
answering your questions.
And so the second era was then
getting large language models
to do what you actually want.
And that's profoundly
more useful.
And that was done
by a second stage
of training to produce so-called
post trained or instruction
tuned models using an extra
training process called
reinforcement learning from
human feedback, which again, I'm
not going to get into the
details of but roughly
you're showing it pairs
of possible responses
to a question or
command and saying
you should choose this one
which gives the answer not
rabbiting on meaninglessly.
And once that was done,
we had the ChatGPT moment
and we had these amazing
tools that you can
use for all sorts of things.
I'll just give one
example that starts
to point into how you can start
to ease-- so easy to research
with these models.
So suppose I want to know a
little bit about climate change
and pollution in my
home country, Australia.
One of the biggest polluters is
AGL, a major energy producer,
so I thought I'd
look up about that.
But they have 168-page
annual report PDF,
and that didn't seem
very fun to read.
So I could give it
to anthropic's Claude
and say, please read the 2022
AGL annual report, the most
recent one that's out there?
What does the AGL report say
about the company's alignment
with IPCC on climate action?
And in a matter of
seconds, it tells me.
It says, that the
company is committed
to aligning our business with
the goals of the Paris Agreement
and the IPCC's 1.5
degrees Celsius pathway.
AGL has set a target of
net zero emissions by 2050.
And it's working on stuff.
It gives some them
particular quotes about it.
Now, this might seem
a little bit too easy
because this is just picking
out and summarizing bits
of the report that's there.
But I could ask it a slightly
more challenging question.
I can then say, are
there any comments
that raise concerns as to
whether AGL is fully committed
to a net zero carbon
emissions future?
And they say yes, there
are a few comments
that raise concerns.
The company's decision to
delay the closure of its Loy
Yang A coal-fired power
station, by two years.
This decision was
made about concerns
about the Australian
electricity grid,
but it's a setback for
Australia's climate goals,
and the company plans to invest
in new gas-fired power stations.
That's cleaner
burning than coal,
but it's still a fossil
fuel and well, it
says it's going to achieve
net zero emissions by 2050,
but it hasn't given
any transparency of how
it's going to achieve that.
So it seems to be
able to do quite
contextual, relevant
understanding
of what things might mean.
And so that's a baby example
with just one document
of this now widely
adopted across industry
and in public goods system
of what's called retrieval
augmented generation,
a model where
we give LLMs access to not
just one document, but tons of
documents.
We have some information need
that we're interested in.
We do a usually
neural network search
to find relevant documents.
The model then reads
these documents,
finds relevant pieces, and
then can answer our questions
about them.
I want to give some
balance in this talk.
On the one hand,
this is fantastic.
On the other hand,
it's so, so important
that you're not naive about
this and expect everything
to be perfect.
I've actually been
doing a bunch of work
with Dan Ho's group
in the law school,
and we've looked quite a
bit at how good these rag
large language model systems are
at actually giving legal advice.
And you don't want
to trust them.
So in a recent paper
for which I actually
contributed very little,
since it was mainly
careful legal analysis of the
results that were returned
by these systems, I
really like these examples
because it's just stunning
what bad understanding
of legal documents these
models are actually saying.
So the commercial
legal RAG system
says when the lender
receives the collateral that
secured the
fraudulent loan, this
is considered a return of
any part of the loan money.
This was established in the
Supreme Court case Robers
versus US.
But actually Robers held
precisely the opposite.
The phrase any part
of the property return
refers to the property
the bank's lost and not
to the collateral.
And then this one says D.M
versus State has been overruled
by Davis versus State.
Also, the case
Millbrook versus US
was reversed by the same
case at a later date.
But actually Millbrook versus
US was a Supreme Court decision.
And so the Nebraska Supreme
Court couldn't overrule it
and didn't even cite it
in the judgment that's
being talked about.
So buyer beware.
If you're just
going to be putting
stuff into your term paper
or legal brief straight out
of a RAG system.
But then we've gone
on a lot from there.
So we're now starting to see
this new era of agentic AI,
where we are now hooking
up these large language
models to systems that can
actually do stuff for us.
And so that's then leading into
new opportunities and dangers
of large language models doing
things in the world around us.
And so that's also
a hopeful area
in which one could hope
to use systems that go out
and perform tasks, as well as
just simply get information
for us.
There are many other
kinds of AI as well.
I've just talked about
large language models,
but generative AI is also
turned up for every other media.
So there's images, and sounds,
and video and things like that.
I'm not really going
to talk about those,
but I did just want to show this
one slide on image generation
research.
And the point I
want to make here,
is really about the role
of University research.
So these days I
presume most of you
have seen some of
the wonderful images
that you can get
from generative AI,
whether it's from tools

or something like that.
The interesting history was that
the idea of diffusion models

was actually
invented at Stanford

Surya Ganguly in 2015.
And so this was a picture

As you can see,
it looks terrible.
I think this is

But you can't really even

But this was the first idea

a few years later.

a 2019 system also

And, well, now we can at least

so progress was made.
And then there was
further work on it

at Berkeley

before it eventually made it out

But the first commercial

This is Midjourney version 1.
I wouldn't want that to

Midjourney version 2

but then things
rapidly progressed.
Some of them looked fairly

but to the modern

So University

we spend a lot of time

And what's just come out

But University research really
underpins these new developments
and often precedes
it by a decade.
So now we're into era 3.
So in the last
year, the new thing
is to say, well, we're going

large language models
to actually move into
models that can think.
And the new buzzword

before it answers the question.
Again, this was a
very simple idea
that proved to have
amazing legs to it.
So even going right back
to the early days, 2022.
If you gave to the model a

1,250 books must be

it just came up with

I had in purple, if you stuck at

Or some phrase like that gave it

a mind hack nudge.

actually comes up

Awesome.

for any of you that
